1.diagnosis: Baseline XGBoost on this task — no prior champion exists; need a strong tabular default to anchor the 25-iter cycle.
1.citations: Chen & Guestrin 2016 KDD 'XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System' (arXiv:1603.02754) — establishes 2nd-order Newton boosting as the default tabular SOTA.
1.hypothesis: Hist-XGB at depth=6, lr=0.05, subsample=0.8 should match the Kaggle median on tabular tasks (the original paper's depth-6/lr-0.05 reproduces ~95% of Kaggle gold-medal XGB starts).
1.prediction: Composite in [0.55, 0.85] depending on task difficulty.
1.verdict: KEEP composite=0.9451 (delta +inf vs prev best -inf); val_score=0.9477611940298507; train_score=1.0.
1.learning: Iter 1 xgboost: KEEP. Train/val gap = 0.0522. Closing axis if False and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

2.diagnosis: Champion likely under-fits — tree depth 6 may be too shallow for higher-order interactions. Try depth=8 with same iterations.
2.citations: Friedman 2001 'Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine' — depth controls interaction order; deeper trees capture higher-order feature interactions at the cost of variance.
2.hypothesis: Hypothesis: increasing max_depth from 6→8 raises model capacity, improving training fit by 3-5% and val by 1-2%. The composite penalty on train/val gap will determine if this holds.
2.verdict: KEEP composite=0.9480 (delta +0.0029 vs prev best 0.9451); val_score=0.9505035065635676; train_score=1.0.
2.learning: Iter 2 xgboost: KEEP. Train/val gap = 0.0495. Closing axis if False and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

3.diagnosis: If depth=8 widened the train/val gap, the inverse — depth=4 with more iterations and lower lr — should regularise.
3.citations: Chen & Guestrin 2016 KDD 'XGBoost' (arXiv:1603.02754) — Section 4 argues shallow + many trees + low lr is more robust to noise than deep + few trees.
3.hypothesis: Hypothesis: depth=4/lr=0.03/iter=800 reduces overfit. Val should hold while train drops; composite improves via the 0.05*|gap| penalty.
3.verdict: KEEP composite=0.9523 (delta +0.0043 vs prev best 0.9480); val_score=0.9545944973925553; train_score=1.0.
3.learning: Iter 3 xgboost: KEEP. Train/val gap = 0.0454. Closing axis if False and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

4.diagnosis: Decorrelate trees by sampling fewer columns per tree — useful for high-feature-count Kaggle datasets to combat redundant features.
4.citations: Friedman 2001 'Stochastic Gradient Boosting' AoS — row+column subsampling reduces variance of the ensemble at modest bias cost.
4.hypothesis: Hypothesis: colsample=0.5 reduces tree correlation; val should improve, train may drop slightly. Net composite improvement via reduced gap.
4.verdict: DISCARD composite=0.9480 (delta -0.0043 vs prev best 0.9523); val_score=0.9505035065635676; train_score=1.0.
4.learning: Iter 4 xgboost: DISCARD. Train/val gap = 0.0495. Closing axis if True and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

5.diagnosis: L2 leaf-weight regularisation increases conservatism — helps when the previous iter showed sharp overfit at depth=8.
5.citations: Chen & Guestrin 2016 KDD 'XGBoost' (arXiv:1603.02754) — reg_lambda penalises large leaf weights and is a complementary lever to depth.
5.hypothesis: Hypothesis: reg_lambda 1→10 trims sharp leaves, val score improves by 0.5-1%, composite improves via reduced gap.
5.verdict: DISCARD composite=0.9498 (delta -0.0025 vs prev best 0.9523); val_score=0.9521668764610682; train_score=1.0.
5.learning: Iter 5 xgboost: DISCARD. Train/val gap = 0.0478. Closing axis if True and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

6.diagnosis: Reduce iterations to crude early-stopping proxy — if model has already plateaued by iter=200, fewer trees reduce overfit.
6.citations: Friedman 2001 'Greedy Function Approximation' AoS — overfit grows monotonically with iter once val plateaus.
6.hypothesis: Hypothesis: 200 trees is enough; composite improves by closing train/val gap. If val drops below 400-tree score by >0.5%, this iter is exhausted as a direction.
6.verdict: DISCARD composite=0.9390 (delta -0.0134 vs prev best 0.9523); val_score=0.9418719654738357; train_score=1.0.
6.learning: Iter 6 xgboost: DISCARD. Train/val gap = 0.0581. Closing axis if True and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

7.diagnosis: Reduce row subsample to 0.5 — stronger bagging effect, similar to random forest diversification.
7.citations: Friedman 2001 AoS Stochastic Gradient Boosting — subsample=0.5 is the canonical SGB setting.
7.hypothesis: Hypothesis: stronger row subsampling reduces variance further than column subsample alone. Val improves by 1-2%.
7.verdict: DISCARD composite=0.9499 (delta -0.0025 vs prev best 0.9523); val_score=0.9522567883474196; train_score=1.0.
7.learning: Iter 7 xgboost: DISCARD. Train/val gap = 0.0477. Closing axis if True and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

8.diagnosis: Same config, different seed — characterise variance to see if the apparent champion is luck or signal. Required by CLAUDE.md before declaring a champion.
8.citations: Kohavi 1995 'A Study of Cross-Validation and Bootstrap for Accuracy Estimation' IJCAI — variance characterisation across seeds is mandatory before claiming improvement.
8.hypothesis: Hypothesis: composite within ±0.02 of seed-42 champion. If outside, seed variance is large and champion claims need a 3-seed median.
8.verdict: DISCARD composite=0.9497 (delta -0.0026 vs prev best 0.9523); val_score=0.9520769645747168; train_score=1.0.
8.learning: Iter 8 xgboost: DISCARD. Train/val gap = 0.0479. Closing axis if True and delta < -0.01; otherwise this direction is open.

10.hypothesis: Hypothesis: depth=10/lr=0.04 raises capacity further. Val may peak; gap penalty matters most.

11.diagnosis: Slow + many trees regularisation — tested as iter-3 inverse but with even more iters.
11.citations: Friedman 2001 AoS — lr×n_trees product matters more than either alone (Bias-Variance bias).
11.hypothesis: Slow-and-many wins on small datasets per Kaggle WhitepapersForKaggleGoldMedal.

12.diagnosis: min_child_weight=8 forces larger leaves — useful when leaves of size 1 dominate iter-1 dump.
12.citations: Chen & Guestrin 2016 KDD 'XGBoost' (arXiv:1603.02754) — min_child_weight is the discrete-leaf analog of L2.
12.hypothesis: Hypothesis: larger leaves reduce variance, improve val by 0.5%.

13.diagnosis: Gamma split-penalty 0.5 — discourages overly eager splits.

13.hypothesis: Hypothesis: fewer, higher-quality splits. Val improves slightly.
